title: Browse Mode and Web Navigation
description: Learn how to navigate web pages and HTML documents using NVDA's browse mode. A practice web page will open automatically when you start a lesson.

steps.instruction: Browse mode is how NVDA helps you read web pages. Sighted users can see a web page laid out visually. NVDA creates a virtual text copy of the page that you can navigate with arrow keys and single-letter shortcuts, as if it were a document. When you press Enter, a practice web page will open in your browser in a separate window. Stay here and finish reading these instructions first. NVDA Coach will remain focused so you can read the next step. When you are ready, press Alt+Tab to switch to your browser. Press Enter to open the practice page and continue.
hints: Browse mode creates a virtual copy of the web page as a text document. Arrow keys move through it just like a regular document.
Single-letter shortcuts like H for heading and K for link only work in browse mode — not in forms or text fields where you need to type.
NVDA switches to browse mode automatically when you open a web page. You will hear 'browse mode' announced when it activates.

steps.instruction: The practice page is now open in your browser. Press Alt+Tab to switch to it. Once you are there, press Down Arrow to begin reading the page. Press NVDA+Shift+C at any time to return here and continue your lesson. Press Enter when you are ready.
hints: Press Alt plus Tab to switch to your browser. Hold Alt and tap Tab once to move to the next open window.
Once in the browser, just press Down Arrow to start reading the page line by line — the same way you read a document.
Press NVDA plus Shift plus C at any time to return to the Coach window and continue your lesson.

steps.instruction: Press Alt+Tab to switch to your browser.
steps.hint: Hold the Alt key and press Tab. This cycles through your open windows. Release both keys when your browser is announced.
hints: Hold the Alt key — to the left of the Space bar — and press Tab once. Release both keys when you hear your browser announced.
If you cycle past the browser, keep holding Alt and press Tab again to continue cycling through open windows.
Once in the browser, NVDA should announce the page title and 'browse mode' to let you know you are in a web page.
steps.successMessage: You switched windows. You should now be in your browser on the practice page.

steps.instruction: You are in the browser. Press Down Arrow to start reading the page line by line.
steps.hint: Just press the Down Arrow key. In browse mode, NVDA reads each line as you move down.
hints: Press the Down Arrow key — the downward-pointing key in the arrow cluster. In browse mode it moves through the page one line at a time.
NVDA reads each line as you move down, just like reading a document. The page content flows top to bottom.
If NVDA is silent, make sure you are focused on the browser window. Try pressing NVDA plus T to confirm which window is active.
steps.successMessage: You are navigating the practice page in browse mode. Arrow keys work just like reading a text document. Well done.

steps.instruction: Web pages are structured with headings, like chapters in a book. Sighted users see headings visually — they are larger or bold. NVDA announces the heading text and its level. In browse mode, press H to jump to the next heading. Shift plus H goes back to the previous heading. Make sure you are in your browser on the practice page. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Press H by itself — just the letter H with no modifier key. In browse mode, single letters are shortcuts, not typed text.
NVDA announces the heading text and its level, for example 'level 2 heading: Getting Started'. The level tells you where it sits in the page structure.
Shift plus H goes backward to the previous heading. The pattern of pressing a key to go forward and Shift plus that key to go backward applies to most browse mode shortcuts.

steps.instruction: Press H to jump to the next heading on the page.
steps.hint: Just press the letter H by itself. In browse mode, single letters are navigation shortcuts, not typed characters.
hints: Press H by itself — just the letter key, no modifier. In browse mode, letter keys jump to page elements, not type characters.
NVDA announces the heading text and its level number. Level 1 is the main title; level 2 is a section; level 3 is a sub-section.
If nothing happens, make sure you are in browse mode in the browser. Press NVDA plus Shift plus C to return to Coach if needed.
steps.successMessage: You jumped to the next heading. NVDA announced the heading text and its level.

steps.instruction: Press H again to move to the next heading.
steps.hint: Keep pressing H to move forward through headings on the page.
hints: Keep pressing H to move forward through all the headings on the page. Each press jumps to the next one.
Scanning headings with H is the fastest way to get an overview of a web page's structure — like reading a table of contents.
If NVDA says 'no next heading', you have reached the last heading on the page.
steps.successMessage: Good. You can press H repeatedly to scan all the headings on a page very quickly.

steps.instruction: Now press Shift+H to go back to the previous heading.
steps.hint: Hold the Shift key and press H to move backwards through headings.
hints: Hold Shift and press H. This is the backward version of the H shortcut — the same pattern applies to most browse mode navigation keys.
NVDA announces the heading you jump back to, including its level and text.
Shift plus a navigation letter always moves backward in browse mode. H and Shift plus H let you scan any page's headings in either direction.
steps.successMessage: You jumped back one heading. Shift plus a navigation key always moves backward. H and Shift+H let you scan any page's structure in seconds.

steps.instruction: Web pages use different heading levels for structure — level 1 is the main title, level 2 is a major section, level 3 is a sub-section. Sighted users can see this hierarchy by the size of the text. In browse mode, you can press a number from 1 to 6 to jump directly to a heading of that level. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Press 1 to jump to level 1 headings, 2 for level 2, and so on up to 6. Use the number row at the top of the keyboard, not the numpad.
Shift plus a number jumps backward to the previous heading of that level — the same forward and backward pattern as other browse mode keys.
Most well-structured pages have one level 1 heading (the main title) and several level 2 headings (main sections). Level 3 headings are sub-sections within those.

steps.instruction: Press 1 to jump to the next level 1 heading on the page.
steps.hint: Press the number 1 from the top row of your keyboard, not the numpad.
hints: Press the number 1 from the top row of the keyboard — the row above the letter keys. Do not use the numpad.
NVDA announces the level 1 heading text. Level 1 is usually the main title of the page.
If NVDA says 'no next heading at level 1', the page only has one level 1 heading or none at all.
steps.successMessage: You jumped to a level 1 heading. This is usually the main title of the page.

steps.instruction: Press 2 to jump to the next level 2 heading.
steps.hint: Press the number 2 from the top row of your keyboard.
hints: Press the number 2 from the top row of the keyboard. Level 2 headings are the main sections of a page.
NVDA announces the level 2 heading text. Keep pressing 2 to move through all level 2 headings in order.
Level 2 headings on a news site might be article titles. On a help page they might be topic names.
steps.successMessage: You jumped to a level 2 heading. These are the main section headings.

steps.instruction: Press 3 to jump to the next level 3 heading.
steps.hint: Press the number 3 from the top row.
hints: Press the number 3 from the top row. Level 3 headings are sub-sections within a level 2 section.
NVDA announces the level 3 heading text. Level 3 headings provide more detailed structure within major sections.
Heading-level navigation is one of the most powerful tools for quickly scanning a web page. Combine H for all headings with numbers for specific levels.
steps.successMessage: You jumped to a level 3 heading. Heading-level navigation is one of the most powerful tools for quickly scanning a web page.

steps.instruction: Links are what you activate to move between web pages. Sighted users can see links as underlined or colored text. In browse mode, press K to jump to the next link — NVDA announces the link text so you know where it goes. Shift plus K goes to the previous link. Tab also moves you forward between links and other interactive elements. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Press K by itself to jump to the next link. NVDA announces the link text — this tells you where the link goes before you follow it.
Shift plus K goes to the previous link. The forward and backward pattern applies here just like with headings.
Tab also moves between links and interactive elements, but it skips plain text. K moves through links only, while Tab includes buttons and form fields too.

steps.instruction: Press K to jump to the next link on the page.
steps.hint: Press the letter K by itself. NVDA will announce the link text.
hints: Press K by itself — just the letter key with no modifier. NVDA announces the link text so you know where it leads.
The link text is the descriptive name of the link. Good link text tells you the destination; vague text like 'click here' does not.
To follow a link, press Enter. To continue scanning without following it, just press K again to move to the next link.
steps.successMessage: You jumped to a link. NVDA announced the link text. To follow a link in browse mode, press Enter.

steps.instruction: Press K again to jump to the next link.
steps.hint: Keep pressing K to move through all links on the page.
hints: Press K again to jump to the next link. Keep pressing K to scan all links on the page in order.
NVDA announces each link's text as you land on it. This lets you quickly scan all the navigation options on a page.
If NVDA says 'no next link', you have reached the last link on the page.
steps.successMessage: Good. You can scan all links on a page very quickly with K.

steps.instruction: Now press Shift+K to go back to the previous link.
steps.hint: Hold Shift and press K to move backward through links.
hints: Hold Shift and press K to move backward. This follows the same pattern as Shift plus H for headings.
NVDA announces the previous link's text as you jump back to it.
K and Shift plus K together let you freely scan all links on a page in either direction.
steps.successMessage: You moved back one link. K and Shift+K let you quickly review all the links on a page.

steps.instruction: Press Tab to move to the next interactive element on the page.
steps.hint: Tab moves you between links, buttons, and form fields. It only visits interactive elements, not plain text.
hints: Tab moves between all interactive elements — links, buttons, and form fields — skipping over plain text content.
Tab is useful when you want to move through only the actionable parts of a page without reading the surrounding content.
The Tab key is on the far left of the keyboard above Caps Lock. Press it by itself — no modifier needed.
steps.successMessage: Tab moved you to the next interactive element. Tab is great for moving between controls in forms and navigating menus.

steps.instruction: Web pages often have forms with text boxes, buttons, and checkboxes. Sighted users can see these visually; NVDA announces what type of field you land on. In browse mode, press F to jump to the next form field. Press E to jump to the next edit field, which is a text box you can type in. Press B to jump to the next button. Press Enter to continue.
hints: F jumps to the next form field of any type. E jumps specifically to text input fields. B jumps to buttons. All use the same forward and backward pattern — Shift plus the letter goes backward.
NVDA announces the field type and label when you land on it — for example 'Search edit' or 'Submit button'.
To type in an edit field, you first need to switch to focus mode. NVDA often does this automatically, or you can press Enter or NVDA plus Space to switch manually.

steps.instruction: Press F to jump to the next form field on the page.
steps.hint: Press the letter F by itself. This includes text boxes, checkboxes, radio buttons, and more.
hints: Press F by itself — just the letter key. This jumps to the next form field of any type on the page.
NVDA announces the field type — 'edit', 'checkbox', 'radio button', 'combo box' — so you know what kind of control it is.
Shift plus F goes to the previous form field. The same forward and backward pattern applies here.
steps.successMessage: You jumped to a form field. NVDA announced what type of field it is.

steps.instruction: Press E to jump to the next text input field.
steps.hint: Press the letter E. Edit fields are text boxes where you type information.
hints: Press E by itself to jump to the next edit field — a text box where you can type.
NVDA announces the field label and the word 'edit' to identify it as a text input.
To type in an edit field, press Enter to switch to focus mode, type your text, then press Escape to return to browse mode.
steps.successMessage: You landed on an edit field. To type in it, you would first press Enter or NVDA+Space to switch to focus mode.

steps.instruction: Press B to jump to the next button.
steps.hint: Press the letter B. Buttons are controls you activate with Enter or Space.
hints: Press B by itself to jump to the next button on the page.
NVDA announces the button's label. Activate any button by pressing Enter or Space while focused on it.
B is the quickest way to find action buttons — like Submit, Search, or Log In — on any web page.
steps.successMessage: You found a button. Buttons are activated by pressing Enter or Space. B is the quickest way to find buttons on any page.

steps.instruction: Browse mode lets you read and navigate pages. But when you need to type into a text box, you need to switch to focus mode. When focus mode activates, your cursor is placed inside the edit box and you can type directly — this is intentional. Focus mode puts the cursor in the edit box so you can interact with form fields. Your keypresses go directly to the application instead of being used as navigation shortcuts. NVDA often switches to focus mode automatically when you Tab into a text field. You can also toggle manually with NVDA+Space (NVDA command). Press Enter to continue.
hints: Browse mode: letter keys are navigation shortcuts. Focus mode: letter keys type characters into a field — your cursor is in the edit box. NVDA plus Space toggles between them.
NVDA usually switches to focus mode automatically when you enter a text field. You will hear 'focus mode' announced, and your cursor lands in the edit box ready to type.

If you are trying to type and letters are jumping around the page instead of appearing in a field, you are in browse mode. Press NVDA plus Space to switch to focus mode and place the cursor in the edit box.

steps.instruction: Try it now. Hold your NVDA key and press the Space bar. NVDA will announce either 'focus mode' or 'browse mode' to tell you which one you are switching to. In focus mode, letter keys type characters into a field instead of navigating the page. In browse mode, letter keys are navigation shortcuts. NVDA often switches automatically when you Tab into a text field, but knowing how to toggle manually with NVDA+Space gives you full control at any time. Press Enter here to continue.
hints: Hold your NVDA key — Insert or Caps Lock — and press the Space bar. The Space bar is the long flat key at the bottom center of the keyboard.
NVDA announces 'focus mode' or 'browse mode' to confirm which mode you switched to. Toggle back and forth a few times to get the feel of it.

If letters are being typed when you expect navigation, press NVDA plus Space to switch to browse mode. If navigation is happening when you expect typing, press NVDA plus Space to switch to focus mode.

steps.instruction: Press NVDA+Space one more time to switch back to the other mode. Hold your NVDA key and press Space, and listen for NVDA to announce the mode name. Remembering this one shortcut means you will always have control over how your keyboard behaves on any web page. Press Enter here to continue.
hints: Hold NVDA and press the Space bar again to toggle back. NVDA announces the mode name each time you switch.
The Space bar is the long flat key at the very bottom of the keyboard. Hold your NVDA key, then tap Space.
NVDA plus Space is one of the most important web browsing shortcuts. Once it becomes a reflex, you will never feel stuck in the wrong mode.

steps.instruction: Well-designed web pages use landmarks — named regions such as navigation, main content, and footer. Sighted users can see these sections at a glance. In browse mode, press D to jump to the next landmark and skip directly to the part of the page you need. Shift plus D goes backward. Pages also have lists of items. Press L to jump to the next list. These shortcuts help you skip to the right part of a page without reading everything. Press Enter to continue.
hints: D jumps to the next landmark region. Common landmarks include: banner (page header), navigation, main (main content), and contentinfo (footer).
L jumps to the next list on the page. Once inside a list, press I to move between individual list items.
Using D to jump to the main landmark skips past repeated navigation links at the top of a page — a major time saver on every site you visit.

steps.instruction: Press D to jump to the next landmark on the page.
steps.hint: Press the letter D. NVDA will announce the landmark type, such as navigation, main, or contentinfo.
hints: Press D by itself. NVDA announces the landmark type — for example 'navigation landmark' or 'main landmark'.
The main landmark contains the primary content of the page. Jumping to it with D skips past repeated headers and navigation menus.
Shift plus D goes backward to the previous landmark.
steps.successMessage: You jumped to a landmark. Landmarks are a quick way to get to the main content area and skip past repeated navigation links.

steps.instruction: Press D again to move to the next landmark.
steps.hint: Keep pressing D to cycle through all landmarks on the page.
hints: Press D again to jump to the next landmark. Keep pressing to cycle through all landmark regions on the page.
NVDA announces each landmark type as you land on it.
If NVDA says 'no next landmark', the page either has no more landmarks or is not well structured with ARIA roles.
steps.successMessage: Good. You can press D repeatedly to move between all the major sections of a page.

steps.instruction: Now press L to jump to the next list on the page.
steps.hint: Press the letter L. Lists contain grouped items like navigation menus, product listings, or bullet points.
hints: Press L by itself to jump to the next list. Lists contain grouped items — navigation menus, bullet points, product listings, and more.
Once inside a list, press I to move between individual list items one at a time.
NVDA announces 'list with X items' when you land on a list, so you know how many items it contains before you start reading through it.
steps.successMessage: You found a list. Once inside a list, press I to move between list items. L and D together cover most of the structural navigation you need on any page.

steps.instruction: NVDA has a powerful dialog called the Elements List that shows you all the headings, links, form fields, or landmarks on the current page in one place. You can filter the list and jump directly to any item. Sighted users can visually scan a page and spot what they need; the Elements List gives you the same overview in one accessible dialog. It is like a table of contents for the whole page. Press Enter to continue.
hints: The Elements List opens with NVDA plus F7. It shows headings, links, form fields, or landmarks depending on which tab you are on.
Inside the Elements List, Tab moves between the category buttons at the top. Arrow keys move through the list of items. Enter jumps to the selected item on the page.
The Elements List is one of the fastest ways to find a specific link or heading on a long page without reading everything.

steps.instruction: Try it now. Hold your NVDA key and press F7 at the top of your keyboard. The Elements List dialog will open and NVDA will announce it. Inside the dialog, press Tab to switch between viewing headings, links, form fields, and landmarks on the current page. Use the arrow keys to move through the list, and press Enter on any item to jump directly to it on the page. It is like an instant table of contents for the whole page — one of the most powerful tools NVDA gives you for web navigation. When you are done exploring, press Escape to close the dialog, then press Enter here to continue.
hints: Hold your NVDA key and press F7 — the seventh function key in the top row. The Elements List dialog opens immediately.
Inside the dialog, Tab moves between the category buttons: headings, links, form fields, buttons, and landmarks. Arrow keys then move through the items in the selected category.
Type a few letters in the filter box to narrow the list to items containing that text. This is very useful on pages with many links.

steps.instruction: Well done. The Elements List is one of the fastest ways to get an overview of any page. Press Enter to continue to the next lesson.
hints: Use the Elements List any time you land on an unfamiliar page and want to quickly understand its structure before reading.
The filter box in the Elements List lets you type a word to narrow the list — for example, type 'contact' to find a contact link on any page.
Remember: NVDA plus F7 opens the Elements List from any web page. It works in most browsers including Chrome, Firefox, and Edge.

steps.instruction: NVDA+Ctrl+F opens the NVDA Find dialog, which lets you search for any text on the current web page. Sighted users can press Ctrl+F in their browser to find text on screen. NVDA's Find dialog works the same way but announces the result through speech. It works in browse mode and in many documents. Press Enter to continue.
hints: NVDA plus Control plus F opens NVDA's own Find dialog. Hold NVDA, hold Control, then press F.
Type the text you want to find, then press Enter. NVDA jumps to the first match and reads the surrounding text.
NVDA plus F3 finds the next match after the first. NVDA plus Shift plus F3 finds the previous match.

steps.instruction: Try it now. Hold your NVDA key, then hold Control, then press F. The Find dialog will open. Type a word that appears on the practice page — such as 'heading' or 'link' — then press Enter to jump to the first match. NVDA will announce the text around the result. Press Escape to close the dialog without searching. When you are done, press NVDA+Shift+C to return here, then press Enter to continue.
hints: Hold NVDA, hold Control, and press F — all three at the same time. The Control key is in the bottom left corner; F is in the middle row of letters.
Type a word you know is on the page, then press Enter. NVDA jumps to the first occurrence and reads the surrounding text.
If no match is found, NVDA says 'not found'. Try a different search term or check your spelling.

steps.instruction: You can also find the next or previous occurrence without reopening the dialog. Press NVDA+F3 to find the next match, and NVDA+Shift+F3 to find the previous match. These work the same way as Find Next in other applications. Press Enter to continue.
hints: NVDA plus F3 finds the next match. NVDA plus Shift plus F3 finds the previous match. F3 is the third function key in the top row.
After opening Find and jumping to the first result, close the dialog with Escape and use NVDA plus F3 to keep moving through subsequent matches.
This shortcut works in browse mode on web pages and in many document viewers — useful any time you are searching for a specific word or phrase.

steps.instruction: Web pages often use tables to display structured information — rows and columns of data such as schedules, pricing, or comparison charts. Sighted users can see the grid layout visually. When your cursor lands on a table in browse mode, NVDA announces 'table with X rows and Y columns' so you know the size of the table. Understanding what kind of command you are using is important here: table cell navigation commands are NVDA commands — they require your NVDA key or a combination that NVDA interprets. They are not Windows commands or browser commands. Press Enter to continue.
hints: NVDA announces 'table with X rows and Y columns' when you land on a table. This tells you how large it is before you start navigating inside it.
Table navigation commands in this lesson are NVDA commands — they only work when NVDA is running and you are in browse mode on a web page.
Not all tables on web pages are data tables. Some pages use tables for visual layout. NVDA will still announce them, but they may not have meaningful row and column structure.

steps.instruction: To move between cells in a table, use Ctrl+Alt+Arrow keys — these are NVDA commands for table navigation. Ctrl+Alt+Right Arrow moves to the next cell in the same row. Ctrl+Alt+Left Arrow moves to the previous cell. Ctrl+Alt+Down Arrow moves to the cell directly below in the same column. Ctrl+Alt+Up Arrow moves to the cell directly above. As you move, NVDA announces the cell content and its row and column position. This works the same way whether you are on desktop or laptop keyboard layout. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Hold Ctrl and Alt together, then press an Arrow key to move through table cells. Right and Left move across a row. Down and Up move through a column.
NVDA announces the cell content, plus its row and column position — for example, 'row 2, column 3, Price: 49 dollars'. This tells you exactly where you are in the table.
These are NVDA commands — Ctrl+Alt+Arrow keys are intercepted by NVDA in browse mode. They do not work the same way outside of a web page table.

steps.instruction: Try it now on the practice page. Navigate to a table using H or Down Arrow to find the section labeled Tables. Once you are inside the table, try pressing Ctrl+Alt+Right Arrow to move to the next cell, then Ctrl+Alt+Down Arrow to move down a row. NVDA will announce the cell content and position as you move. When you are ready to continue, press NVDA+Shift+C to return here and press Enter.
hints: Hold Ctrl and Alt at the same time — both with your left hand — then press Right Arrow to move to the next cell in the row.
Hold Ctrl and Alt, then press Down Arrow to move to the cell directly below in the same column. NVDA announces the row and column number.
If the table has headers, NVDA announces the column or row header label as you move — for example 'Monday, column 1' or 'Price, row 3'.

steps.instruction: A reminder about command categories: Ctrl+Alt+Arrow for table navigation is an NVDA command. The regular Tab key and Arrow keys are Windows commands that work anywhere. H, K, D, and other single-letter shortcuts are NVDA browse mode commands that only work in browse mode on web pages. Keeping track of which commands belong to which category helps you understand why some keys work in some places but not others. Press Enter to continue.
hints: NVDA commands require NVDA to be running and active. Tab and Arrow keys are Windows commands — they work in any application.
Browse mode single-letter shortcuts like H and K only work when you are in browse mode. If they are not working, you may be in focus mode — press NVDA plus Space to check.
Ctrl+Alt+Arrow for tables is an NVDA command that only activates inside a table in browse mode. Outside a table, Ctrl+Alt+Arrow may do something different or nothing at all.

steps.instruction: Excellent work — you have completed the Browse Mode and Web Navigation chapter! You now know how to navigate web pages using headings, links, form fields, landmarks, lists, the Elements List, NVDA Find, focus mode, and table navigation. These skills let you navigate any web page quickly and independently. When you are ready, press NVDA+Shift+C to open the lesson picker and continue to the next chapter: Object Navigation — where you will learn to reach any control on screen, even ones that Tab cannot get to. Press Enter to finish.
hints: The next chapter is Object Navigation. It teaches you to reach any element on screen — even controls that Tab and browse mode cannot access.
Browse mode quick reference: H for headings, K for links, F for form fields, D for landmarks, L for lists, Ctrl+Alt+Arrow for table cells. Shift plus any of these goes backward.
NVDA Coach saves your progress automatically. All completed lessons are marked when you return.
